title.en: Legal Name Change in India — Affidavit, Newspaper Ads and Gazette, Step by Step

answerBox.en: A legal name change in India takes three steps: a notarised name-change affidavit (₹20–100 stamp paper), publication in two newspapers (one English, one regional — ₹500–1,500), and publication in the Gazette of India (₹1,100–1,700 typical fees). The full process runs 4–8 weeks, after which every ID — PAN, Aadhaar, passport — can be updated.

en.title: Step 1 — the name-change affidavit
en.summary: Old name, new name, reason — sworn before a notary.
en.detail: The affidavit states your old name, the new name, your address, and the reason (marriage, divorce, astrology/numerology, spelling correction, personal choice — all are acceptable). Execute it on **₹20–100 stamp paper** (state-dependent) and get it **notarised** or sworn before an Oath Commissioner. This affidavit is the foundation document every later step and ID office will ask for. Our ₹199 service drafts it correctly the first time.

en.title: Step 2 — publish in two newspapers
en.summary: One English daily + one regional-language daily.
en.detail: Publish the prescribed announcement — old name, new name, address, affidavit date — in **two newspapers**: one English daily and one in the local language of your state. Cost is typically **₹500–1,500** for both classified insertions. Buy several original copies of each paper; the Gazette application and some ID offices want originals of the full page carrying the ad, with the masthead and date visible.

en.title: Step 3 — Gazette of India notification
en.summary: The official publication that ends all argument.
en.detail: Apply to the **Department of Publication, Govt. of India** ([egazette.gov.in](https://egazette.gov.in) / dop.nic.in) with the prescribed proforma, affidavit, newspaper originals, photos, ID proof and the fee (**₹1,100–1,700** for ordinary publication; state gazettes are an alternative for some purposes). Publication takes **3–6 weeks**; you then download the gazette copy carrying your entry. For government employees, the gazette route is mandatory; for passports after marriage, the affidavit + marriage certificate route often suffices without a gazette — check the specific office's rules.

en.title: Update every ID in the right order
en.summary: Gazette in hand, sweep PAN → Aadhaar → passport → banks.
en.detail: With gazette + affidavit + newspaper ads, update: **PAN** (incometax.gov.in correction), **Aadhaar** (UIDAI centre — name changes need supporting proof), **passport** ([passportindia.gov.in](https://www.passportindia.gov.in) reissue), then voter ID, driving licence, bank accounts, insurance, property records and educational-board records where applicable (boards have their own correction windows and rules). Keep a folder of 10+ copies of everything — every office keeps a set.

rows.label: Total, typically
rows.note: Plus ID-update fees (PAN free, passport reissue fees apply)

en.footnote: Fees are indicative and change — verify the current Gazette fee schedule on egazette.gov.in before paying. Beware agents charging ₹10,000+ for what is a straightforward three-step process you can do directly.

en.q: Is the Gazette publication compulsory for a name change?
en.a: Not always. Government employees must use the gazette route, and it is the strongest universal proof. But many offices accept less for specific cases — passport name change after marriage commonly needs only the marriage certificate and a joint affidavit. When in doubt, or for a complete change of name, do the gazette: it ends all future argument.

en.q: How long does the whole process take?
en.a: The affidavit is same-day, newspaper ads appear within a week, and gazette publication takes roughly 3–6 weeks from a complete application — so 4–8 weeks end to end. ID updates after that proceed in parallel at each office's own pace.

en.q: Can I change my child's name?
en.a: Yes — a parent/guardian executes the affidavit and follows the same newspaper + gazette process, with both parents' consent documents typically required. School-board records have their own correction procedures and deadlines, so start with the board's rules if certificates are the goal.

en.q: After marriage, must I take my spouse's surname legally?
en.a: No — it is entirely optional. If you do adopt it, the marriage certificate plus a joint affidavit handles most updates (passport included) without a gazette. If you later revert after divorce, the full affidavit-ads-gazette route is the clean path.

en.q: My documents have different name spellings. Same process?
en.a: Yes — minor spelling mismatches (Mohd/Mohammed, missing surname) are fixed with a "one and the same person" affidavit for small cases, or the full name-change process where an authoritative single spelling is needed across IDs. The affidavit is the ₹199 document that solves most of these.

author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
reviewer: Adv. Arjun Nair, Bar Council of Kerala, KER/0973/2012
